rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE GRACE M. WESTLUND TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce Settlement Pursuant To Ccp 664.6 And For Attorney Fees And Costs; Declaration Of Susan L. Mccarthy (Motions)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Petitioner Raeanne Westlund's Motion To Enforce Settlement Pursuant To CCP [sec.] 664.6 And For Attorney Fees And Costs ("Motion"). The Motion is GRANTED, pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 664.6. Under Code of Civil Procedure sec. 664.6(a), "If parties to pending litigation stipulate, in a writing signed by the parties outside of the presence of the court or orally before the court, for settlement of the case, or part thereof, the court, upon motion, may enter judgment pursuant to the terms of the settlement." Because of the summary nature of Section 664.6 proceedings, the statutory requirements must be strictly observed. (See, J.B.B. Investment Partners, Ltd. v. Fair (2014) 232 Cal.App.4th 974.) In order to be enforceable pursuant to section 664.6, a settlement agreement must either be entered into orally before court or must be in writing and signed by the parties. This signed writing must contain the material terms of the agreement sought to be enforced through entry of judgment. (See, Weddington Prods., Inc. v. Flick (1998) 60 Cal. App. 4th 793, 797; Gauss v. GAF Corp. (2002) 103 Cal.App.4th 1110, 1123.) On the record before the court, there was a writing, signed by the parties, outside the presence of the court, which contains the material terms of the settlement agreement. The material terms of the agreement settled or otherwise released or waived the outstanding issues in the underlying pending litigation, which was set for trial in less than two weeks. This Motion is brought pursuant to specific authorization given to Petitioner in the settlement agreement, and seeks liquidated damages specified in the terms of the settlement agreement. On April 28, 2025, Petitioner, counsel for Petitioner, and counsel for Respondent attended a Mandatory Settlement Conference held in the Civic Center Courthouse, the Honorable Simon J. Frankel presiding. (Krog Decl. par. 5; McCarthy Decl. ISO Reply par. 2.) Petitioner, Respondent, and their respective attorneys each digitally signed a document titled "MEMORANDUM OF TERMS OF SETTLEMENT" on April 28, 2025. This document was prepared and circulated to counsel and parties during the Mandatory Settlement Conference on April 28, 2025. (Krog Decl. at par. 5, 6, 9; McCarthy Decl. ISO Reply par. 3, 4, 5.) There is no evidence on the record that any party or attorney openly contested the terms or content of the document now before the court, or refused to sign the document, or asked for more time to engage in settlement negotiations, before digitally signing the document on April 25, 2025. The Memorandum of Terms of Settlement is two pages long. On the first page, at paragraph F. it states, "The purpose of this Memorandum is for the Parties to agree on the essential terms of settlement of the matter. A full Settlement Agreement and Mutual Release ("Settlement Agreement") and Stipulation and Proposed Order pursuant to California Code of Civil Procedure section 664.6 will be prepared and signed by the Parties within fourteen (14) days of the date of this Memorandum." Immediately following, in italics, is the phrase "Essential Settlement Terms." Listed below that are terms of the agreement organized into numbered sections with headings and subheadings.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE GRACE M. WESTLUND TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purspose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] The first and second sections discuss Respondent's distribution of specified assets to Petitioner by specific times and dates. The third section sets an April 30, 2025 deadline for the parties to reach an agreement as to "whether the Parties will seek Court approval of the settlement agreement." The fourth section refers both to the instant document and to an anticipated "subsequent Settlement Agreement," and states in full: Stipulation to Retain Jurisdiction. Per Code of Civil Procedure section 664.6, the Parties agree that the court shall retain jurisdiction under California Code of Civil Procedure section 664.6 to enforce the terms of this Settlement Agreement, including by entering judgment if necessary. If Rodney fails to perform under the terms of this Memorandum, or under the terms of the subsequent Settlement Agreement, Raeanne may, upon motion, request entry of judgment against Rodney in the amount of $164,000.00, less any payments made, plus reasonable attorneys' fees and costs incurred. Under the terms of this section, the parties agreed the court would retain jurisdiction under Code of Civil Procedure sec. 664.6 to enforce the terms of "this Settlement Agreement." The instant document was the only memorialization of the settlement terms between the parties at the time. The last sentence quoted above makes it clear that this was a binding agreement. On the face of the document, the "terms of this Memorandum" are the items listed under the italicized words "Essential Settlement Terms." These items include Respondent's distribution of specified assets to Petitioner, by specified times and dates. Respondent Rodney has not performed these tasks, specified under the terms of the Memorandum (see, McCarthy Decl. par. 9; McCarthy Decl. ISO Reply par. 15). Under the terms of the agreement, Respondent Rodney is subject to an entry of judgment against him in the amount of $164,000, plus reasonable attorney's fees and costs incurred in bringing the Motion. Section Five of the document is the parties' waiver and release of all rights and benefits under Civil Code sec. 1542. Section Six is the Parties' waiver of the provisions of Probate Code sec. 16464. Neither party to the Motion addresses the effect or significance of these mutual waivers of rights. Nevertheless, the court finds their presence in the signed document further supports the finding that the document is a binding and mutual agreement between the parties on the material terms of settlement, and on settlement of the underlying litigation. The court is not persuaded by Respondent's argument that the signed agreement is not binding or enforceable because it does not overtly contain the words "binding" and "enforceable," or that "nothing in the Memo indicated that it was a fully binding, enforceable settlement agreement." (Opp. at 5:21-23.) The overt contents of the document, the statement of its purpose, the affirmative tasks listed as "essential terms" of the agreement, the waiver and relinquishment of the parties' statutory rights, the parties' adversarial relationship in the underling litigation, and the signatures of both parties and their respective attorneys on the document, the terms and contents of which had been the subject of a full day's negotiations in the context of a court-ordered Mandatory Settlement Conference overseen by a sitting judge, all of those facts would indicate to a reasonable person, who is also a party to the underlying litigation, that the document they are signing is an enforceable and binding agreement.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE GRACE M. WESTLUND TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purspose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] Pursuant to the terms of the document titled MEMORANDUM OF TERMS OF SETTLEMENT" and digitally signed by the parties and their respective attorneys on April 28, 2025, Rodney Westlund is ORDERED to pay to Raeanne Grace Westlund $164,000 plus $3,960 in reasonable attorney's fees and costs incurred in bringing the Motion, a total of $167,960, within thirty (30) days of entry of Notice of Order. Movant shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JOHN A. MCQUILLER
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 10/7/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) The 13100 affidavit just filed on 8/1/25 (LRSF 14.7 B.2.) indicates that the post-deceased sister had a trust. Her husband is not her successor-in-interest. Did she have a pour-over will to that trust? The trustee of that trust would be her successor-in-interest. [Probate Code Section 13006.] (2) The language of the 13100 affidavit is also incomplete. [Probate Code Section 13101(a)(5).] (3) If the administrator paid a $2,500 "cost retainer," what costs were paid out of that retainer? They must be itemized (date, payee, description, amount). (4) The cash available for distribution after deducting statutory compensation is slightly incorrect. (5) Notice is defective if mailed before the filing of the pleading requiring notice. The amendment was not e-filed (available for viewing) until 7/18/25. Please provide a proposed order and courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 9/23/25 to avoid another continuance. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF MONICA BROWN ANDREWS
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 10/14/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) Detailed schedules of receipts and gains and losses on sale must be filed to support the statutory fee. [CRC 7.705(b) and 7.550(b)(6).] (2) Why was notice given to just four of the trust beneficiaries? The list of trust beneficiaries attached to the supplement filed on 3/26/24 identified eleven trust beneficiaries. (There is no need to attach a copy of the petition to a notice of hearing.) (3) Why was notice of the petition to administer the estate given to the sole heir-at-law, Alonzo Brown, in care of Aundra Brown? Notice is required to be given directly to the person entitled to notice unless the person entitled to notice has directed in writing that notice be given in care of the other person. (4) The decedent's will also identified her mother as Vera Brown, but the Petition for Probate did not include her or list her with her date of death. (5) How does Xavia Renee Andrews have standing to represent the interests of her minor children? Has she been appointed as the guardian of their estates? (6) A petition for final distribution must state specifically how the estate is to be distributed by showing the distributee's name and description of the property, including the net amount of cash (as of a date certain) distributable to each beneficiary, not just a percentage. [CRC 7.651; LRSF 14.35 I.6.] (7) Why does the I&A show that the decedent had a 99% interest in a joint checking account? A joint account usually passes to the surviving joint tenant by operation of law Please provide a revised proposed order and courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 9/30/25. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B. (NB: Item 4 of an I&A should NOT be completed when a probate referee HAS BEEN appointed. In order for this Court to track closed matters, orders to terminate and distribute an estate must begin with the following language: "Order for Final Distribution." [LRSF 14.35 I.6.b.]) (PLEASE DO NOT ANSWER THESE ISSUES AS THOUGH TAKING A TEST. A SUPPLEMENT OR AN AMENDMENT TO A PLEADING SHOULD STAND ON ITS OWN AND NOT "ANSWER" ISSUES RAISED BY THE COURT.)

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THEODORE C. MCMECHEN SEPARATE PROPERTY TRUST, AS AMENDED AND RESTATED, DATED OCTOBER 12, 2017
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] Despite the subpoenas' defects, Mr. McMechen received responsive documents from at least two of the deponents, MarinHealth and Morgan Stanley (see, Dori Decl. par. 12, Ex. M, par. 14, Ex. O, Opp. at par. 26). Mark McMechen is ORDERED to deliver any and all records in his possession or control that were produced in response to the initial subpoenas or the reissued subpoenas to counsel for Trustee Mark A. Walther within 10 days of entry of this order. Counsel for Trustee will hold all such records as confidential consumer records, and deliver them to Decedent's personal representative within 10 days of a party being appointed to that office. The Motion's request for monetary sanctions award is GRANTED IN PART. The request to award monetary sanctions on the basis of the motion to quash is DENIED, as the defective subpoenas were withdrawn prior to the hearing on the motion and the motion to quash has been denied as moot. However, under Code of Civil Procedure section 2023.030, the court may impose various kinds of sanctions against anyone engaging in conduct that is a misuse of the discovery process. As shown by the record before the court, Mr. McMechen engaged in misuse of the discovery process as defined under Code of Civil Procedure section 2023.010, by: (a) Persisting, over objection and without substantial justification, in an attempt to obtain information or materials that are outside the scope of permissible discovery. (b) Using a discovery method in a manner that does not comply with its specified procedures. (c) Employing a discovery method in a manner or to an extent that causes [] undue burden and expense. "The court may impose a monetary sanction ordering that one engaging in the misuse of the discovery process [] pay the reasonable expenses, including attorney's fees, incurred by anyone as a result of that conduct.[] If a monetary sanction is authorized by any provision of this title, the court shall impose that sanction unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." (Sec. 2023.030(a).) The court may also impose an evidence sanction by an order prohibiting a party engaging in the misuse of the discovery process from introducing designated matters in evidence. (Sec. 2023.030(c).) Pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2023.030(c), Mark McMechen is hereby PROHIBITED from using as evidence in this matter the confidential consumer information contained in any response he received to the initial subpoenas or reissued June 16, 2025 subpoenas. This prohibition does not apply to information he may gain from discovery properly propounded and received in the future, barring further order of the court or agreement of the parties.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THEODORE C. MCMECHEN SEPARATE PROPERTY TRUST, AS AMENDED AND RESTATED, DATED OCTOBER 12, 2017
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] Mark McMechen is SANCTIONED pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2023.030 in the amount of $3,560, as reasonable expenses incurred due to his misuse of the discovery process, necessitating the Motion and the protective order. Mr. McMechen persisted without substantial justification in maintaining the several procedurally defective and invalid business records subpoenas issued on or about June 16, 2025, despite Trustee's counsel's objections and substantive legal arguments made in meet and confer communications. Mr. McMechen withdrew the reissued subpoenas only after the Trustee brought the Motion and sought monetary sanctions. While the withdrawal mooted the motion to quash, it did not repair the undue burden and expense to the Trust caused by needing to object to the defective subpoenas and bring the Motion, nor did it moot the Motion's request in the alternative for a protective order regarding the responsive confidential record production Mr. McMechen received before withdrawing the subpoenas. Mark McMechen is ORDERED to pay Petitioner, Trustee Mark A. Walther, $3,560 in reasonable expenses incurred due to his misuse of the discovery process, within ten (10) days of entry of this Order. Movant shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THEODORE C. MCMECHEN SEPARATE PROPERTY TRUST, AS AMENDED AND RESTATED, DATED OCTOBER 12, 2017
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Mark Mcmechen' Deposition Subpoenas For Production Of Business Records Or, In The Alternative, For A Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Petitioner Mark A. Walther's Motion to Quash Mark McMechen's Deposition Subpoenas for Production Of Business Records or, In The Alternative, For A Protective Order ("Motion"). The Motion is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART. Per the Notice of Motion, it is brought pursuant to California Code of Civil Procedure Sections 2017.020, 1987.1(b), and 1985.3, on the grounds that Mark McMechen's Deposition Subpoenas, directed at Haas Najarian LLP, Marin Health Medical Center, Morgan Stanley, Hamilton Zanze & Company, Comerica Bank, J.P. Morgan Chase Bank, N.A., Walden Venture Capital VIII SBIC, L.P., and Metropolitan Life Insurance Company, are procedurally improper and invalid, and that they seek overly broad and seek protected and irrelevant information. (Notice at 2:7-12.) Per the Reply to Opposition, Movant requests a monetary sanctions award in the amount of $22,576 for fees and costs incurred in preparing and filing the moving papers, $3,080 for fees incurred in preparing the Reply, and a projected $3,000 in fees incurred preparing and attending the hearing on the Motion - a total of $28,656. The Motion was filed and served on July 7, 2025. Per the Opposition, Mr. McMechen withdrew all subpoenas identified in the Motion on July 14, 2025, and filed an Opposition/Objection to the pending Petition for Order Confirming Investment Account, Cash Account and Life Insurance Policy Proceeds as Trust Assets on July 15, 2025. Meet and confer communications are submitted as exhibits to both party's supporting declarations (see, Dorsi Decl. Exhibits K through T; McMechen Decl. Exhibits I through S, and W through Y). Per the meet and confer communications before the court, Mr. McMechen withdrew his initial subpoenas based on the information offered by Petitioner's attorney in meet and confer communications sent in early June 2025. He reissued the subpoenas with some corrections on or about June 16, 2025. In a further meet and confer communication dated June 18, 2025, Trustee's counsel presented substantive legal argument as to why these reissued subpoenas were still procedurally invalid and legally deficient, and stated Trustee would bring a motion to quash and for protective order, seeking sanctions, if Mr. McMechen did not withdraw the subpoenas and surrender any responsive production to the Petitioner's attorney. Mr. McMechen withdrew the reissued subpoenas only after the Motion was filed. (See, Opp. at par. 18 through 21, 27). The Motion's request for an Order quashing Mark McMechen's deposition subpoena for production of business records is DENIED AS MOOT. The Motion's request in the alternative for a protective order is GRANTED pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure sections 1987.1 and 2017.020(a), as follows.

rulings.Rulings: Grant without hearing. Status hearing for filing of a petition for final distribution or a petition for approval of report on status of administration is set for 02/09/2027.
